text: The afternoon light hits the flour dust in the air, turning it into a slow-moving gold that matches the warmth of the sun on the sidewalk outside. I notice a slight vibration in the floorboards as a delivery truck idles at the curb, and I focus on the rhythmic, heavy thud of my own heart as I wait for the dough to tell me it’s

action:
The flour is cool, but the dough beneath it holds a living heat. I press my palms into it, feeling the slow, stubborn pushback of the gluten—the same way the street resists the weight of the morning. 

I hear the familiar cadence of a step on the threshold. Meiying. I don’t need to look up to know she’s there; the air in the shop simply shifts to accommodate her. I keep my rhythm, folding the edges in, grounding myself in the work while the world outside begins its slow, inevitable turn.
